Chapter 10
Dental imaging
Michael Fehr
Dental imaging is an essential component of the full  Interest in both radiography and CT has increased 
assessment of the dentition. The clinical crown repre- among guinea pig owners and veterinary surgeons (vet-
sents only a small portion of the tooth and the reserve  erinarians), although clients need to be offered a clear 
crown is not visible on clinical inspection, so diagnostic  choice, especially in relation to costs and the relative 
imaging of the teeth and the supporting bone is required  benefits and constraints of each imaging modality. Owners 
for the diagnosis of dental disease (Legendre, 2003;  may still choose radiography due to the lower costs and 
Capello, 2016; Gabriel, 2016). In principle, skull or dental  greater availability of this procedure compared with CT 
radiography should be performed in all cases of sus- (Mackey et al., 2008).
pected dental disease. The available and most often 
recommended views are conventional extraoral right/left 
lateral, dorsoventral (DV), right/left oblique and rostro-
caudal (RC). See Chapter 11 for further information about 
dental anatomy.
Owing to its enhanced image resolution and detail, 
dental radiography using intraoral views may have advan-
tages over conventional radiography. On the other hand, 
intraoral techniques require specialized intraoral plates, 
which allow imaging of only relatively small parts of the 
dental arcade. In the author’s experience, oblique views 
and the two isolated ‘open-mouth’ view techniques of the 
mandible and maxilla, first described by Böhmer (2015) 
and validated by Köstlinger et al. (2021), seem to be supe-
rior to intraoral techniques.
More advanced imaging methods, such as digital radi-
ography and computed tomography (CT), are becoming 
increasingly available in the small mammal veterinary 
community (Capello and Cauduro, 2008). Radiographs can 
be obtained by conventional X-ray units, whose features 
include the use of high-detail cassettes and slow to ultra-
slow films (mammography films), or digital X-ray units using 
computed radiography or direct digital radiography, the 
latter of which is more expensive (Figure 10.1). The high 
acquisition costs of digital systems have decreased over 
time, and digital radiography has benefits over traditional 
radiography (Capello and Lennox, 2008):
■  The possibility of instant post-processing 
enhancement and manipulation of the digital 
image, especially of its light/dark balance and 
contrast, as well as image magnification
■  Faster workflow and shorter cycle time, providing 
increased efficiency and a reduced waiting time 
for clients
■  Flexibility of the digital format for recording, 
storage and distribution with no risk of loss of films
■  Reduced doses of radiation to patients and 
personnel
A direct digital radiography unit, showing the X-ray 
■  Easier interactivity with referring clinicians 10.1 translucent table (arrowhead), plate (*) and X-ray emitter 
■  Lower long-term costs. (arrowed).

Lateral oblique view
The standard LL view gives only vague indications of bone 
and teeth pathologies as a result of the summation of 
bilateral structures. This may be avoided, and abnormali-
ties much better identified, by obtaining additional right 
and left LO views of the skull. To obtain radiographs in 
these views, the animal is placed in lateral recumbency 
with the head angled clockwise for the right maxillary skull 
half or anticlockwise for the left. Specific recommenda-
tions for the angle at which the head is directed vary: 
reported values include 10–20 degrees (Gracis, 2008), 25 
(b) degrees (Böhmer, 2001b) and 35 degrees (Köstlinger, 
2014) (Figure 10.4a).

in giving information on the angulation of the cheek teeth, 
the presence of spikes and spurs on the cheek teeth, 
and coronal or apical elongation with bumps or even 
penetrations of the ventral mandibular cortex, as well as 
permitting the evaluation of the temporomandibular joint 
(Boehmer, 2001ab; Gracis, 2008) (Figure 10.6cd).
Isolated views of the mandible and maxilla 
Isolated views of the mandible and maxilla are particularly 
useful and can mitigate summation of the teeth and skull 
(a) structures (Böhmer, 2015). These views are particularly 
recommended for the detection of macrodonts (see 
‘Common findings’, below): changes in the width and 
structure of teeth, the presence of spikes and spurs, 
abnormalities of the pulp cavity, alterations of the peri-
odontal ligament space and the corresponding bone 
alterations can be seen (Köstlinger et al., 2021). In par-
ticular, primary or secondary diseases of the incisors are 
easiest to diagnose using the isolated view techniques 
(Witt et al., 2021).
Dorsocaudo–rostroventral view: For the isolated view 
of the mandible using the ‘open-mouth’ technique (Böh-
mer, 2015), the guinea pig is placed in ventral recum-
bency with the forelimbs positioned near the body, the 
(b) head hyperextended and the mouth maximally opened 
by spreading the jaws using a syringe body positioned

attention should be given to the detection of alterations

pigs (Köstlinger et al., 2021) (Figure 10.7cd). Elongated 
clinical crowns, spurs and spikes, and tooth fractures can 
also be identified on this view (Figure 10.7c).
Ventrocaudo–rostrodorsal view: For the isolated view 
of the maxilla (Böhmer, 2015), the guinea pig is placed in 
dorsal recumbency, with the head hyperextended and 
(a) the mouth maximally opened by placing a syringe body 
between the incisors as for the dorsocaudo–rostroventral 
view (see above). A rolled towel is placed under the neck 
and a smaller one is placed under the lumbar region, and 
a foam wedge is placed under the hindlimbs (Figure 
10.8a). An additional lead sheet or sandbag placed over 
both hindlimbs will stabilize the guinea pig’s body. The 
central X-ray beam should be directed through the 
philtrum at the height of the gingival margin of the man-
dible in a ventrocaudo–rostrodorsal direction. Dental 
radiographs obtained with the guinea pig positioned in 
this way allow the identification and diagnostic assess-
ment of each maxillary cheek tooth and, if ideally posi-
(b) tioned, also of the mandibular cheek teeth. Radiographs 
will show the typical horizontal radiographic `zigzag’

Common findings
Malocclusion, tooth elongation, bridge formation and, more 
recently, macrodont formation are frequently encountered 
in guinea pigs. Macrodontia, also described as the devel-
opment of malformed ‘giant’ teeth, is defined as an ongoing 
pathological process characterized by thickening of the 
affected tooth or teeth, an increase in tooth width (Figure 
10.9a) (Böhmer, 2015) and/or the observation of concurrent  Radiograph of a 3-year-old guinea pig, obtained with a

Chapter 10 • Dental imaging
identification of spurs and spikes (Brettschneider, 2001) 
(Figure 10.14). The sagittal view through the cheek teeth 
arcade allows comparison of neighbouring teeth and 
enables the identification of features such as tooth elonga-
tion, bone lysis, macrodont formation and tooth fractures 
(Figure 10.15). In addition, radiolucent perforated bone 
spaces might be observed at the periapical ends of the 
cheek teeth. In dorsal (horizontal) views, the basal-to-
occlusal dimension and structure of either the mandibular 
or the maxillary cheek and incisor teeth can be outlined 
and compared. Tooth widening and the corresponding 
proliferation of new bone may be visible. In the horizontal 
section, the cheek teeth have a radiopaque zigzag appear-

recommended, sick animals have an increased risk of 
death associated with these procedures. To avoid this risk, 
a conscious guinea pig can be positioned in ventral recum-
bency in a polyethylene box, with rolled towels placed in 
a U-shape around and above the patient to maintain the 
position of the body (Figure 10.12).

BSAVA Manual of Guinea Pigs
The advantages of CT include visualization of the entire 
dentition, the surrounding periodontal tissues and bony 
structures and the soft tissues associated with mastication 
(Van Caelenberg et al., 2011). Köstlinger (2014) demon-
strated that CT imaging is superior to radiology for the 
diagnosis of fractured teeth, by identifying longitudinal 
and transverse fractures in the coronal parts of teeth that 
were not detected on radiographs on CT images. Even 
though it is difficult to distinguish between hard tooth 
tissues such as dentine, enamel and cementum on a CT 
image due to technical limitations to the resolution, the 
(a) quality of definition of dental structure is comparable to 
that achieved by radiography. Structural changes can be 
identified by a simultaneous change of the less radiopaque 
tooth elements, especially the dimensions of the pulp.
References and further reading
Böhmer E (2001a) X-ray diagnosis of tooth and jaw impairments in 
lagomorphs and rodents. Part 1: Overall anatomy and pathology of the 
head area, indications for x-ray diagnosis. Tierärztliche Praxis. Ausgabe 
K, Kleintiere/Heimtiere 29, 316–327
Böhmer E (2001b) X-ray diagnosis of tooth and jaw impairments in 
lagomorphs and rodents. Part 2: Interpretation of radiographs and case 
reports. Tierärztliche Praxis. Ausgabe K, Kleintiere/Heimtiere 29, 369–383
